The Robinhood breach underscores how attackers are increasingly exploiting trusted public figures rather than exchange infrastructure itself, using social media to manufacture legitimacy and rapidly funnel speculative capital into fraudulent onchain tokens before the deception is uncovered.

data.title: Kimi K3 has torn apart the entire Silicon Valley.
data.description:
Author: Astronaut Ape; Source: GeekPark

On July 22nd, four things happened on the same day.

White House Office of Science and Technology Policy Director Michael Kratsios publicly accused Moonshot AI's Kimi K3 on X of allegedly "mass-distilling" American models.

OpenAI President Greg Brockman, in an interview with Bloomberg, admitted that K3 "is a very good model, without a doubt."

Meanwhile, nearly 200 Silicon Valley startups jointly wrote to the Trump administration, warning that if Chinese open-source models are banned, "hundreds of companies will die instantly." Meanwhile, Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang stated explicitly in an exclusive interview with Axios that American companies "should absolutely be allowed to use Chinese models."

Four voices, four directions, all pointing to the same trigger. This trigger was the release of Kimi K3 by the Chinese AI company Dark Side of the Moon on July 16th. If the shockwaves ignited by DeepSeek in early 2025 primarily impacted Wall Street's faith in AI infrastructure investment, then the wave ignited by K3 addresses a more fundamental questionŌĆöwhat should the US AI industry do when China can open up model weights at a near-cutting-edge level? The increasingly powerful Kimi K3, in just one week, has torn apart Silicon Valley and Washington. 01 Not "Another Chinese Model" Let's start with Kimi K3 itself. With 2.8 trillion parameters, a MoE (Hybrid Expert) architecture, 16 experts are activated at a time out of 896, and a context window supporting 1 million tokens, K3 is currently the world's largest open weighted model. The full weighted model will be publicly released on July 27th. On the Artificial Analysis Intelligence Index, K3 scored 57, ranking thirdŌĆöbehind only Anthropic's Fable 5 (60 points) and OpenAI's GPT-5.6 Sol (59 points), surpassing Claude Opus 4.8 (56 points). In terms of front-end code capabilities, K3 reached number one on the Arena leaderboard within 24 hours of its release, surpassing all leading US models. Technically, K3 introduces several interesting architectural innovations. Kimi Delta Attention (KDA) is a hybrid linear attention mechanism that achieves a 6.3x decoding speedup in contexts with millions of tokens. Attention Residuals (AttnRes) allow each layer to selectively retrieve information from any earlier layer, rather than accumulating it uniformly like traditional residual connections. Combined with Stable LatentMoE and quantized perceptual training starting from the SFT stage, K3 achieves approximately 2.5x better overall scaling efficiency compared to its predecessor, K2. However, what truly worries the US is not the benchmark scores, but the simultaneous occurrence of "performance + price + openness." K3's API price is $15 per million output tokens. This is considered expensive among Chinese competitorsŌĆöDeepSeek V4 costs only $0.87, and Zhipu's GLM-5.2 costs $4.4. However, compared to closed-source models in the US, this price is less than one-third of Fable 5. A model approaching cutting-edge level, offering services at a price far lower than competitors, and disclosing all weightsŌĆöthese three factors combined make K3 the Chinese AI product with the biggest impact on Silicon Valley since DeepSeek. And this didn't just appear out of thin air. Before K3, Kimi's series of AI models had already quietly infiltrated Silicon Valley's production chain. SpaceX is acquiring Cursor, a code tool for approximately $60 billion, whose Composer 2 core runs on Kimi K2.5. DoorDash's CTO, Andy Fang, publicly stated that the company has delegated "low-level work" to Kimi K2.6. Thinking Machines uses K2.5 to generate early post-training data for its new model, Inkling. The release of K3 is less a debut of a new model and more a public confirmation of the fact that Chinese open-source AI has been "embedded in the production process" in Silicon Valley. 02 The Torn Silicon Valley If it were just about "a new Chinese model achieving high benchmark scores," the story would end there. But in the week following K3's release, the truly explosive event wasn't the technical reviews, but the reaction it triggered in the United States. In shortŌĆöfor the first time, Silicon Valley witnessed a public, intense, almost camp-like confrontation over whether "open-source models are a good thing or a bad thing." The first camp was the White House and policy hawks. Kratsios's accusations on X were quite harsh, stating that Moonshot "developed a sophisticated internal platform to distill US models at scale and could quickly switch between multiple access methods to avoid detection." Treasury Secretary Bessent also signaled a similar sentiment the previous day, mentioning that sanctions might be considered if Chinese models were found to contain "watermarks" of US models. Both houses of Congress are also pushing forward legislation targeting unauthorized distillation. The logic of this camp is clearŌĆöK3's capabilities may be backed by fabricated illegitimate means, requiring policy and legal tools to address. The second camp consists of US closed-source laboratories. OpenAI's attitude is the most intriguing. President Brockman acknowledged K3's capabilities but shifted the focus to OpenAI's infrastructure investment advantages, arguing that open weighted models are not truly "free" because large-scale deployment still requires expensive hardware. He estimated that China is still about four months behind the US in overall model capabilities. But what better represents this sentiment is the long tweet written by Dean Ball, OpenAI's Director of Strategic Futures, the day after the K3 launch. Ball called open-source models "inherently decelerationist" because they erode profit margins in cutting-edge labs, reduce sustained investment in AI infrastructure, and ultimately slow the development of the most powerful models. He even predicted that a world dominated by open weighted models would lead to "AI communism"ŌĆöa future he considers "a dystopian nightmare." He also suggested that the Trump administration's best strategy was to "create massive regulatory risks" for Chinese open-source models, using FUD (fear, uncertainty, and doubt) to drive American companies away. Undoubtedly, this blatant critique of the "calculations behind the ideology" immediately sparked enormous controversy. Because the third campŌĆöAmerican startups and open-source supportersŌĆöcompletely rejected this view. A new organization called the "Little Tech Association," whose members include Proton, Replit, and Y Combinator, stepped forward. They organized nearly 200 companies to jointly write to the White House, with only one core messageŌĆöif the Chinese open-source model is banned, it won't be Chinese companies that die, but American entrepreneurs. Suhail Doshi, founder of the startup Particle, put it more bluntly: "Hundreds of companies will die instantly. This is fantastic for AnthropicŌĆöwe'll all have to pay for Anthropic's services." The most influential voice in this camp comes from Nvidia founder Jensen Huang, the head of the world's largest AI chip supplier, who gave a completely opposite assessment to the White House in an interview with Axios. Jensen Huang said that Wall Street misunderstood DeepSeek first, and now it's misunderstood K3. His logic is simpleŌĆöfree AI is good for chips, good for data centers, and good for hardware. Cheaper open-source models will allow more people and businesses to use AI, which will increase, not decrease, the demand for computing infrastructure. "There is no scenario where China drives out American companies," Huang said. "Zero possibility." He also refuted the claim that open-source models "leave backdoors for China," arguing that companies can customize and control models within a secure sandbox. In his view, openness makes AI safer, not more dangerousŌĆöbecause external researchers can review models, discover vulnerabilities, and build defenses. "If everything becomes a single model, a single attack surface, a single source of failure, the world will be much more fragile." 03 The Debate on "Slowdownism" Leaving aside political noise, Dean Ball's argument that "open source is slowdownism" actually touches upon a real industrial logic problem that deserves careful analysis. His reasoning is as follows: the development of cutting-edge models often requires billions of dollars in investment. If a Chinese lab can provide near-equivalent open-source alternatives at extremely low cost, the profit margins of closed-source labs will be compressed. Reduced profits mean less reinvestable capital, and the capital market will lower the end-user value of these companies, further suppressing financing. The combination of these two effects will ultimately slow down the development of cutting-edge models. AI researcher Nathan Lambert acknowledges in his analysis that, from a purely economic perspective, Ball's logic holds trueŌĆöopen source does indeed have a "slowing-down" effect on cutting-edge labs at the economic level. However, he also points out that this effect is not enough to prevent OpenAI and Anthropic from becoming the world's most valuable companies. The market is growing, and even if open-source models take a share, closed-source models still possess a strong moat in terms of brand trust, reliability, and service ecosystem among enterprise customers. But the voices of opponents are equally sharp. Several commentators point out an ironic factŌĆöOpenAI, where Ball works, and the entire modern AI field, are themselves built on open source. The Transformer architecture is a published paper, PyTorch is open-source software, and the research foundations and technology sharing that made OpenAI's breakthroughs possible all occurred before OpenAI "closed the door behind it." The current criticism of open source as "slowdownism" is like building a skyscraper using someone else's open-source tools and then telling later adopters that the doors can no longer be opened. Some even call Ball's argument "digital McCarthyism," arguing that closed-source labs are trying to use national security narratives to solidify their market position. At its core, this debate is a question of path selection. If large-scale models represent a new industrial infrastructure capability, should they be widely supplied and globally exported like electricity and the internet, or strictly controlled by a few institutions like nuclear technology? American closed-source labs see a zero-sum gameŌĆöChina's openness is eroding their commercial space. But from another perspective, the rapid progress of open-source models is also doing somethingŌĆöcontinuously reducing the cost of using AI, enabling more developers and companies that previously couldn't afford cutting-edge models to access these capabilities. These two perspectives are not mutually exclusive, but they lead to drastically different policy conclusions. 04 The Chinese Model's "Own Pace" The capital market has already given its first reactionŌĆöin the week of K3's release, the Philadelphia Semiconductor Index fell 12.5%, its biggest drop in 15 months. Nvidia, AMD, and Broadcom all declined. Even China's own AI concept stocks were not spared; Zhipu fell 28% in Hong Kong, and MiniMax fell 16%. Jensen Huang said the market has made a mistake again. But this time, the market's panic is not about the benchmark scores of a particular model, but about a path conflict that has become unavoidable. Taking a step back, the root of all the current debates in Silicon Valley lies precisely in the goal chosen by the American AI industry itselfŌĆöextreme accelerationism. The logic of this path is clear: AGI is the single, highest-priority goal; to support this goal, a closed-source business model that maximizes profits is employed; extreme capital density is used to build extreme computing power; and ultimately, a leap in capabilities that leaves the rest of the world behind is achieved. The funding scale, valuation multiples, and computing power investment of OpenAI and Anthropic are all products of this path. Dean Ball's concern about "open source slowdown" essentially means that if this profit engine is weakened by the open-source model, the fuel for extreme acceleration will decrease. However, while extreme accelerationism creates competitiveness, it will inevitably give rise to another path. While American labs possess extreme profits and extreme capital, Chinese AI companies are not in the same environment. They do not have the same level of capital density. But life has its own methodsŌĆöunder constraints, they will naturally grow their own signposts. The shift in strategy undertaken by Yang Zhilin, founder of Moon's Dark Side, is the most vivid illustration of this path. In October 2023, he stated very clearly in a conversation with GeekPark: "Closed source is the only path to a Super App," and "We have no plans to open source for the time being." At that time, Moon's Dark Side aimed to create a C-end super app using a closed-source approach. However, after the DeepSeek shockwave in 2025, Yang Zhilin made a decisive adjustment. K2, with its trillion-parameter parameters, was open sourced, and K2.5 quickly became popular in the open-source communityŌĆöits revenue in the 20 days following its release exceeded the total revenue for 2025, and individual subscription orders surged more than 80 times month-over-month. K3 continued along the open-source path, reaching 2.8 trillion parameters. This was not a random strategic shift. In March of this year, Yang Zhilin, as the only Chinese independent founder of large-scale models invited to speak at NVIDIA GTC 2026, presented a very clear technical roadmap in his "How We Scaled Kimi K2.5" presentationŌĆönot by piling on parameters and data to catch up with closed-source frontiers, but by replacing all three "foundations" that had been used for nearly a decade in the Transformer era. The optimizer Adam (2014), attention mechanism (2017), and residual connection (2015) were all replaced by three alternatives, all open-sourced. Musk commented that it was "impressive," and former OpenAI co-founder Karpathy remarked that people's understanding of the groundbreaking Transformer paper might not be sufficient. Four months later, K3 essentially delivered on the roadmap outlined in that presentationŌĆötoken efficiency, long context, and agent cluster collaboration were all implemented simultaneously. At a Reddit AMA in late 2025, when asked about the gap between themselves and their American counterparts, the Dark Side of the Moon team said, "We do have fewer GPUs than our American counterparts, but we've squeezed the performance of each card to its limit." When co-founder Zhou Xinyu was asked about OpenAI's cash burn, his answer was more relaxed: "We don't know either, only Sam knows. We have our own rhythm." "Our own rhythm"ŌĆöthese five words are perhaps the most important footnote to understanding the entire path of AI in China. It's not just Dark Side of the Moon. The DeepSeek team has created a model of equivalent quality with fewer resources. Zhipu is continuously iterating its GLM series. DeepSeek V4 was released in April this year, GLM-5.2 went live in early July, and the WAIC conference was just held in Shanghai. This isn't a story of "another Chinese model has arrived," but rather a continuous, intensive, multi-point concurrent release of capabilities. Each leverages its unique strengthsŌĆöinnovation in underlying architecture, optimization of engineering efficiency, and construction of an open-source ecosystemŌĆöto forge a different path to the forefront without the extreme capital density of the US. OpenAI's Brockman stated that China is only about four months behind in overall capabilities. This figure doesn't indicate a lag, but rather that both paths are simultaneously approaching the forefront in their own ways. This won't be a winner-takes-all story. If large-scale models truly represent a new industrial foundation capability, their destiny isn't to be monopolized by a single company or country, but rather to be used by more people. The US follows a capital-driven, closed-source-focused path, with AGI as its ultimate goal. China's path is efficiency-driven, utilizing open source as a tool, and prioritizing broader capability output. Both paths have their advantages and costs, but together they are raising the overall level of AI. On July 27th, the full weights of K3 will be publicly released. At that time, any developer worldwide can download, fine-tune, and deploy this 2.8 trillion parameter model. That will be the moment when the competition truly intensifies. However, perhaps even more important than July 27th is this: from DeepSeek to Kimi, the long-term coexistence of the two AI enginesŌĆöChina and the USŌĆöas the core driving forces of global AI is no longer a definitive prediction, but an inevitable reality. K3 is simply the latest proof of this.

data.title: Deng Yu wins Fields Medal: The problem he solved has stumped AI.
data.description:
Author: Meng Xing, Partner, Five Sources Capital

Today, the Fields Medal is all over the news, and everyone should have seen the names of Wang Hong and Deng Yu. Interestingly, Wang Hong's videos have been circulating everywhere these past two days, while Deng Yu has been much quieter, with almost no media exposure.
What I've been thinking about repeatedly these past few days is Deng Yu. Not because he's a Chinese mathematicianŌĆöbut because the 125-year-old problem he, along with Zaher Hani and Ma Xiao, tackled happens to hit a blind spot in today's AI development that is becoming increasingly crucial, yet almost everyone avoids it. It's even more worthy of discussion than "yet another Fields Medal" itself.
This problem is called Hilbert's Sixth Problem.
I first heard this concept from Liu Ziming, a newly appointed assistant professor at Tsinghua University's Institute for Intelligent Manufacturing, who said his vision was to solve Hilbert's sixth problem. At the time, I didn't understand what kind of problem it was that was so important, or why the first five problems were skipped. Later, after learning more, I found that textbooks summarize it as "establishing physics in an axiomatic way." That's true, but it's too grandiose, so grand that it's hard to see how it relates to today's AI startups and investments. To be more specific, what it aims to do is: starting from the fundamental laws describing individual microscopic particles, rigorously and step-by-step derive the macroscopic equations describing gases and fluids. I prefer to translate it in plain language: Once we understand every basic unit of the world, why should we assume that simply accumulating enough of these units will automatically reveal a higher level of world law? Knowing how a molecule moves is not the same as knowing how a gas flows. Knowing how a protein folds is not the same as knowing how a cell reacts to a drug. Knowing how a consumer fills out a questionnaire is not the same as knowing where a market will ultimately go. Even knowing how parameters are updated in a small model doesn't equate to truly understanding generalization, grokking, and emergence in large models. These questions seem to belong to physics, biology, social sciences, and machine learningŌĆöcompletely unrelated. But they share the same underlying structure: How can correctness at one scale become correctness at another? This is the true significance of Hilbert's sixth problem for the AI ŌĆŗŌĆŗera. It is also likely the very problem that many so-called "world models" today haven't yet solved. How can a group of tiny balls become a stream of water? Let's first discuss what Deng Yu's team actually did. Imagine a box containing countless tiny, hard balls. Each ball obeys only the most basic Newtonian mechanics: it moves at a constant velocity in a straight line when not colliding with anything, and changes direction according to the conservation of momentum and energy upon impact. That's all. If there were only ten balls in the box, it would be easyŌĆönumber each ball, record its position and velocity, and calculate step by step. However, in a real gas, a mass of air contains approximately 10┬▓┬│ molecules. Even if you had unlimited computing power, capable of precisely tracking the position and velocity of every single molecule, this method of description would offer almost no help in "understanding a gust of wind." Because when we observe a gust of wind, we don't care whether the 784 quadrillionth molecule is currently moving left or right. What we really want to know is: How dense is the gas here? Which direction is it flowing overall? What is the temperature? What is the pressure? Thus, facing the same physical world, humanity has developed three completely different descriptive languages: Newtonian spherical dynamics (microscopic) ŌåÆ Boltzmann equations (mesoscopic) ŌåÆ Euler equations / NavierŌĆōStokesŌĆōFourier equations (macroscopic) At the bottom is the microscopic level: Newtonian spherical dynamics, tracking the position and velocity of every particle. In the middle is the mesoscopic level: Boltzmann equations. It no longer numbers particles, but rather counts "how many particles are moving at a certain velocity near a certain position". The top layer is macroscopic: the Euler equation and the NavierŌĆōStokesŌĆōFourier equation. At this layer, even the complete velocity distribution is discarded, leaving only macroscopic quantities such as density, average velocity, and temperature. From an informational perspective, this is an extremely radical compression: from a massive state of 10┬▓┬│ particles, compressed into a few continuously changing fields in space. But the most difficult part of this process is not "averaging." Averaging is easy. The real challenge lies in proving this: After discarding most of the microscopic details, how can you guarantee that those discarded elements won't suddenly return at some point in the future and disrupt the macroscopic results? In other wordsŌĆöif I only retain density, velocity, and temperature, can the density, velocity, and temperature of the next second truly be calculated solely from these current macroscopic quantities? Or, after taking one step, do I have to go back to the bottom layer and re-examine the history of all molecules? In physics and mathematics, this problem has a specific name: closure. A macroscopic theory is only truly independent and valid if it achieves closure; otherwise, it's not a theory, but merely a nice-looking interface for microscopic simulations. Please remember this word. It will appear repeatedly later. The difficulty isn't the collisions themselves, but the "memory" they leave behind. The biggest obstacle in moving from Newtonian particles to the Boltzmann equations is the correlation between particles. If all particles were completely independent, everything would be easy. The problem is that they collide. A and B collide, and their states are no longer independent. Then B collides with C, and C collides with D. After a while, A might meet D again through another path. At this point, although A and D have no direct initial relationship, they are already subtly connected by a collision chain: A ŌåÆ B ŌåÆ C ŌåÆ D ŌåÆ ŌĆ” (The correlation spreads, branches, and rejoins along the collision chain). This is like throwing a stone into water, with ripples spreading outwards. What starts as a small relationship between a pair of particles can, over time, weave into an extremely complex network of relationships: the collision chain branches, the branches rejoin, and particles that have collided will collide again. The Boltzmann equation simplifies the problem significantly thanks to a key approximationŌĆöoften called **"molecular chaos"**: Two particles about to collide can be approximated as independent before the collision. This doesn't mean they have no history. They certainly have past collisions and may have indirectly influenced each other in several ways. What Boltzmann truly needs is that as the number of particles increases, their size decreases, and the gas becomes sufficiently rarefied, the influence of these intricate historical correlations on the current collision eventually becomes negligible. In 1975, Oscar Lanford first rigorously proved this. This "proof" isn't about running a large simulation or observing whether a group of small balls move like gas. He proved a true limit theorem: when the number of particles is infinitely large and the particles themselves are infinitely small, the statistical distribution of a Newtonian hard-sphere system converges to a solution to the Boltzmann equation. However, Lanford's result has a fatal limitation: it only lasts for an extremely short period, roughly a fraction of the average collision time. The problem isn't that real gas no longer obeys the Boltzmann equation after this period, nor is it that particles can only collide a finite number of times. The problem isŌĆöhis proof method itself went awry. To extrapolate the present of a particle, you have to trace back to who it collided with; to figure out that collision, you have to trace back to who it collided with before. With each step forward in time, the number of possible collision histories explodes. In a very short time, "so many complex collisions" is a low-probability event, and this low probability can suppress the explosive growth of collision histories; but as time goes on, the number of combinations of histories expands exponentially, and traditional proof methods can no longer sum up these terms. This dilemma will be familiar to anyone who has done long-range rollout: a model that predicts accurately within one or ten steps does not mean that you can roll it ten thousand steps and still control the errors, bifurcation, and correlations. Lanford proved that this path was correct, but he only took a very short step. The work of Deng Yu's team in 2024 broke through this time barrier that had held them back for nearly half a century. They proved that as long as the Boltzmann equation itself, as the target, has sufficiently regular solutions within this time period, then the rigorous derivation from the Newtonian hard-sphere system to the Boltzmann equation can be extended to any given finite time, no longer confined to Lanford's limited timeframe. The phrase "arbitrarily long" here needs careful interpretation. It doesn't mean they unconditionally proved the end of the universe. The precise meaning is: given a finite time period, as long as the Boltzmann equation doesn't reach a singularity, explode, or lose its regularity within that time, the microscopic particle system will convergentally follow it throughout that entire period. The second paper in 2025 represents another step forward. From the Boltzmann equation to the fluid equations of Euler and NavierŌĆōStokesŌĆōFourier, the mathematical community has accumulated a wealth of work. The real bottleneck lies in the fact that previously, only a very short section of the first bridge from Newtonian particles to Boltzmann could be traversed, so the two theoretical segments had never truly connected. The 2025 paper extended the 2024 results to two-dimensional and three-dimensional periodic spaces, and then combined them with existing fluid limit theories, creating the first complete chain: Newtonian hard sphere system ŌåÆ Boltzmann equation ŌåÆ compressible Euler equation / incompressible NavierŌĆōStokesŌĆōFourier equation. Therefore, the simplest understanding is: in 2024, they lengthened the bridge from "microscopic to mesoscopic"; in 2025, they connected the entire bridge from "microscopic to mesoscopic to macroscopic". Of course, the boundaries must be clearly stated: they are solving the classic version of Hilbert's sixth problem, "deriving the fluid equations from Newtonian mechanics, through Boltzmann theory," rather than axiomatizing all of physics. It has a definite scope of applicationŌĆörare gases, hard sphere models, specific scaling, and the existence of regular solutionsŌĆöfar removed from complex liquids, long-range interactions, chemical reactions, and real boundary conditions. And I believe that "clearly stating the boundaries" is itself extremely important. This is precisely the biggest difference between mathematics and many AI narratives: a truly credible model not only tells you in what conditions it holds true, but also honestly tells you in what conditions it does not. We will return to this point later. The essence of world models is not about remembering more, but about knowing how to forget. The reason I feel Deng Yu's team's work is so relevant to AI is because it brings to the forefront a fact we often underestimate: The world often requires completely different representations at different scales. Moving from particles to fluids is not as simple as scaling up a particle model a trillion times. At the macroscopic scale, even the variables describing the world change: position and velocity at the microscopic level, probability distribution at the mesoscopic level, and density, flow rate, and temperature at the macroscopic level. Moving to the next scale is not about increasing quantity, but about changing the language. Today, when people talk about world models, they often subconsciously understand them as an ever-expanding end-to-end simulator: more data, larger parameters, longer videos, farther rollouts, more agentsŌĆöas if the underlying simulation is detailed enough, the higher-level patterns will automatically emerge, and we can automatically know how to use it. Hilbert's sixth problem tells us: this step is never free. A truly useful world model, besides making predictions, must also find the **"sufficient states"** of the world at a certain scale: this set of states must be simple enough that it doesn't need to record all the details; and complete enough that it can independently determine what will happen next. Density, velocity, and temperature are a set of sufficient states in the fluid worldŌĆöthey discard almost all molecular-level details, yet can still describe a gust of wind, a stream of water, or even a shock wave in the air. So the real brilliance of world models may not lie in remembering more, quite the oppositeŌĆöit lies in knowing what can be safely forgotten and proving that forgetting it won't lead to mistakes. The word "safe" is key. Models not only need to compress information, but also must know whether those compressed variables will return to change the outcome through feedback, correlation, or long-term evolution. This, ultimately, brings us back to closure. To summarize the difficulty of this layer with a few sharper questions: Which underlying differences are actually irrelevant? Which relationships must be preserved? Which micro-perturbations will be averaged out? Which micro-correlations will be amplified into macro-structures? And most importantlyŌĆöis the set of high-level variables we retain sufficient to independently determine the future? If the answer is "yes," then we have obtained a true macro-world model. If we have to go back and check the complete state of each molecule at every step, then what we have is not a theory, but merely a micro-simulator wrapped in a skin. The following sections are the key points I want to discuss: this "cross-scale" problem appears exactly in some of the hottest AI fields today. AI for Drug Discovery: From combining a target to curing a person, how many layers are in between? Drug development is the most typical example. Today's AI can already perform quite well on many single-point problems: predicting protein structure, predicting the binding of molecules and targets, generating candidate compounds, estimating the effect of a gene perturbation on cell expression, and predicting the response of a certain cell to a drug. These are all important. However, the ultimate value of a drug is not determined at the molecular level, but rather in the patient. There lies a very long chain of steps: Molecules ŌåÆ Target Binding ŌåÆ Pathway Influence ŌåÆ Cellular Alteration ŌåÆ Tissue Remodeling ŌåÆ Organ Action ŌåÆ Patient Therapy and Side Effects. A molecule's ability to bind firmly to a protein doesn't guarantee it will alter cell fate as expected. Altering cell fate doesn't mean it can repair tissue. Even if effective in the target organ, it doesn't guarantee it won't be toxic in another. With each layer up the chain, new variables and feedback emerge. Knocking out a gene might compensate through another pathway; inhibiting a protein might cause cells to simply change their metabolic pathway; killing some tumor cells might actually create selective pressure on the remaining cells. Biological systems are rarely linear. Therefore, the most difficult aspect of drug development is often not predicting more accurately at a particular level, but rather: how to reliably transmit interventions at one scale to higher scales. I first heard this concept from Professor Feng Xiqiao, my country's first academician in cell dynamics, and this is precisely the true significance of today's virtual cell research. The goal of AI Virtual Cell is not simply to reconstruct a cell atlas, but to learn the dynamic responses of cells under different conditions and perturbationsŌĆöresearchers have explicitly stated that a unified representation needs to be established across measurement methods and scales, and perturbation predictions should be used to validate the model. However, please note: even if we truly possess a very good virtual cell, we are still far from a virtual patient. Because in the eyes of a tissue model, a cell reverts to its underlying "particle" state. Cells communicate, compete, migrate, and change each other's states. No matter how well a cell model is made, piling up a million such models will not automatically yield a reliable tissue. A macroscopic level is often just a microscopic level below. Life is not a one-off micro-to-macro, but a series of ascending scales. Each step forward presents a completely new Hilbert's sixth problem. Agent Society: Simulating an individual and simulating a society are two different capabilities. Another highly typical direction is Agent Society. A number of companies, including Aaru and Simile, along with a wealth of academic work, are attempting to simulate consumer, voter, corporate, policy, and social behavior using a large number of generative agents. Aaru defines itself as a multi-agent population simulation system; Simile proposes to expand step by step from individuals, long-term journeys, and interactions to the entire market and even society. The academic community has also been able to build an agent for each of over a thousand real participants based on in-depth interviews and test their ability to reproduce the participants' attitudes and behaviors. The first step in this type of work is to make each individual agent sufficiently like the person it representsŌĆöcan it reproduce a person's preferences, experiences, speaking style, and decision-making habits? Doing this step well is already very valuable. But there's a huge logical leap between "one person" and "a group of people": "If we can accurately simulate every individual, then putting enough individuals together will naturally allow us to accurately simulate society." This sounds self-evident, but it's almost identically flawed to "knowing the Newtonian motion of every particle means knowing the motion of fluids." Suppose a model can very accurately answer "Would you buy this product?", but adding up 100,000 such answers only yields a faster, cheaper market research report. It's not a market model. Because the market is never the sum of a hundred thousand independent answers. It also depends on: whether consumers will change their minds after seeing others buy; whether competitors will lower prices; who the platform algorithm allocates traffic to; how the influence of KOLs and friends spreads; how scarcity changes attractiveness; andŌĆöwhether the original predictions still hold true after companies act based on the predictions themselves. A influences B, B influences C, and C, in turn, influences A. These feedbacks and correlations are the most crucial part of a social system. It's full of "collisions" and "re-collisions," just like in a gaseous environment. Therefore, the real challenge for Agent Society is never whether "a single agent resembles a person," but rather: When countless agents begin to influence each other, does the entire system still resemble a real society? To evaluate this type of company, I think it should be broken down into at least four layers, and these four layers are progressive: First layer, individual effectiveness: Does the agent resemble the person it represents? Second layer, interaction effectiveness: Does the interaction between two agents resemble the interaction between two real people in a real situation? The third layer, group effectiveness: how closely the resulting distribution of opinions, prices, norms, and behaviors after extensive interaction approximates the real market and society. The fourth layer, intervention effectiveness: whether the model can predict how the real world will change when we change prices, policies, products, or communication structures. The fourth layer has truly enormous commercial value. However, the vast majority of benchmarks remain at the first layer, or at most the second. This precisely exposes a very common misconception in AI investment: the model's benchmark occurs at a lower level, while commercial value occurs at a higher level, and the greatest risk lies between these two levels. I previously discussed grokking with Liu Ziming. He described his research problem as a kind of "AI version of Hilbert's sixth problem," and I think this analogy is very accurate. Grokking is a highly counterintuitive phenomenon: a model might memorize the training set perfectly very early on, but still perform poorly on the test set; after a very long period of training, it suddenly shifts from "memorizing" to "understanding," and its generalization ability dramatically improves. Liu Ziming and his colleagues' research approach was to first understand the internal mechanismsŌĆöthe dynamics of representation learning and phase diagramsŌĆöin very small, very simple models, and then study how these mechanisms extend to larger models as the data scale, parameter scale, regularization, and training conditions change. This is certainly not the same as the particle-to-fluid transition, but it is structurally highly similar. Because neural networks also have different scales: At the micro level, it's about how each parameter changes, how each gradient is updated, and how each neuron and feature is formed. At a higher level, it's about whether the model has formed structured representations, whether it has shifted from memorization to generalization, whether it suddenly starts groking, and whether it exhibits emergent ability after its scale crosses a certain threshold. Today, we're used to using scaling laws to describe macroscopic results: more parameters, more data, more computing power, and the loss drops according to a certain pattern. But scaling laws mainly tell us "how the results change," not "what actually happens internally." It's somewhat like this: we've observed how temperature and pressure change, but we haven't been able to truly deduce them from molecular collisions. Therefore, AI's own Hilbert's sixth problem might be phrased as follows: Can we explain, starting from parameters, gradients, and training dynamics, why macroscopic capabilities like representation, generalization, inference, and emergence emerge? And can we determineŌĆöwhen can the mechanisms observed in small models be extrapolated to large models, and when are they merely illusions at a finite scale? This is very important because there's a very common methodology in AI research: first, discover a phenomenon in a toy model, give a nice explanation, and then assume it holds true in models with hundreds of billions or trillions of parameters. But scaling from a small model to a large model is itself a scale shiftŌĆöit needs to be proven, not just through analogy. The reverse is also true: observing a nice empirical curve in a large model doesn't mean we understand the underlying mechanism. Just like seeing fluid flowing doesn't mean you've derived Navier-Stokes from Newtonian mechanics. Weather, robots, and organizations are all hitting the same wall. The same problem appears in a whole host of other areas. Weather and Turbulence. It's impossible to analyze every tiny eddy, so processes smaller than the grid scale must be compressed into a closure model. Machine learning is being used to learn how these subgrid processes feed back into large-scale flow fieldsŌĆöbut a closure that fits beautifully on training data can quickly become unstable in long-term physical simulations, even violating energy conservation, symmetry, and generalization under extreme conditions. Autonomous Driving and Robotics. Predicting the next frame of a video is not the same as understanding a long-term, interactive physical world. From pixels to objects, from objects to scenes, from scenes to the intentions of other participants, and from single-vehicle behavior to urban traffic, it's a series of continuous scale transitions. A single-vehicle model might be accurate at every step, but when placed in a system composed of numerous human drivers, pedestrians, and other autonomous vehicles, the macroscopic results can be completely different. AI in Enterprises. We often hear a deduction about ROI: if each programmer's efficiency increases by 30%, then the company's R&D efficiency will increase by 30%. But organizational output is never a simple sum of individual outputs. Writing more code may increase the review burden; faster requirements may exacerbate prioritization chaos; easier communication among individuals may lead to more meetings; improvements in local efficiency can be completely wiped out by organizational coordination costs. This is the same kind of error as "smarter agents necessarily form a smarter agent society." From individual capabilities to system output, there are organizational structures, workflows, incentives, information flows, and accountability mechanisms in between. At this point, it's worth reiterating that core judgment: Scaling a simulator is not the same as simulating a scale. Making the simulator larger doesn't mean you've entered a new scale. A million particles are not a fluid model. A million cells are not a human body model. A million consumer agents are not a social model. Increased scale may bring new patterns, but it won't automatically tell you what those patterns are, much less prove their reliability. Every world model has its own Hilbert's Sixth Problem. The significance of Hilbert's Sixth Problem lies not only in its existence for 125 years, nor simply in the fact that someone finally took a giant leap forward. It lies in its profoundly insightful worldview: The world is not a plane with a single resolution, but rather composed of layers of different scales. At different scales, the important objects differ, the descriptive variables differ, the effective laws differ, and even the effective boundaries differ. Microscopic laws do not automatically become macroscopic laws simply by scaling up the systemŌĆöa genuine theoretical leap is required, necessitating new representations, new assumptions, and a clear understanding of which details can be discarded and which correlations must be retained. Today, we are using AI to simulate more and more worlds: molecules, cells, materials, weather, robots, human behavior, markets and societies, and even AI models themselves. But each direction, in the end, will likely encounter the same question: Can our success at one scale be carried over to the next? If we cannot prove this, we may only possess a very strong local predictor, rather than a true model of the world. We might be able to predict a protein with remarkable accuracy, yet still not know why a patient recovers; we might be able to simulate a consumer with remarkable accuracy, yet still not know why trends form; we might be able to clearly understand how a small model groks, yet still not know why intelligence emerges on a larger scale. A larger model does not equal a larger world. A true world model is not about cramming all the details into an infinitely large neural network until it replicates the entire universe. It needs to know at every scale: what must be remembered, what can be forgotten, what will disappear in the average, what will be amplified in interactions, what can emerge upwardsŌĆöand why. The ability to translate between these scales is where the truly difficult and valuable step in creating a world model begins.

data.title: SK Cross-Market Arbitrage: A Frenzy of Arbitrage Across US Stocks, Korean Stocks, and Crypto Markets
data.description:
Author: Jae, PANews

As the demand for memory chips from major US AI companies continues to grow, semiconductor giant SK Hynix has become the focus of global capital, taking advantage of the situation to list on Nasdaq and issue ADRs (American Depositary Receipts) to further broaden its financing channels.

Just two weeks after its listing, SK Hynix's US ADRs (stock code SKHY) have maintained an average premium of around 30% relative to its domestic South Korean ordinary shares, exhibiting a rare phenomenon of "different prices for the same stock."

The emergence of high premiums has also made cross-market arbitrage of SK Hynix a "gold mine," with an arbitrage game reaching its climax last week, spanning from South Korean spot stocks and US ADRs to the crypto market. This article will analyze five arbitrage strategies operating across multiple markets, including US stocks, South Korean stocks, crypto exchanges, and DeFi.

The triple "fault" fuels arbitrage premiums. The surge in cross-market arbitrage for SK Hynix is ŌĆŗŌĆŗessentially the result of a combination of factors: blocked two-way conversion channels, spillover of sentiment from the crypto market, and misaligned trading time zones. The structural primary reason for the traditional market premium lies in the lack of interconnectivity between US ADRs and Korean spot ADRs. On July 9th, SK Hynix issued 177.9 million ADRs at $149 per share, raising $26.5 billion, making it the largest ADR issuance in the history of a foreign company. The issuance was oversubscribed by more than 7 times, with top global institutions such as Baillie Gifford and Coatue securing a total of $5 billion in cornerstone shares. However, the issued shares only represent approximately 2.5% of the company's total share capital, making the circulating shares inherently scarce. In principle, every 10 SKHY shares correspond to 1 Korean stock. However, due to the initial closed-loop conversion channel, the two cannot be freely exchanged. In a normal ADR mechanism, arbitrageurs can buy low-priced domestic common stock, convert it to ADRs, and then sell it at a higher price in the US market, ultimately eliminating the price difference. However, SKHY's ADRs are not created by depositing existing shares, but by issuing new shares. The Korea Depository & Clearing Corporation (KDS) stipulates that applications for two-way conversion between SKHY common stock and ADRs cannot be initiated until July 29th. Before then, the market only supports the one-way operation of canceling ADRs to exchange for Korean stocks; the reverse ADR supply channel has not yet been opened. On this single-track path, the huge demand for US stock funds has squeezed into the scarce ADR circulating supply, coupled with the market heat of the AI ŌĆŗŌĆŗstorage sector, continuously pushing up the SKHY premium. This is similar to the long-standing premium of TSMC ADRs, only with Hynix's volatility being more pronounced. While the market expects the premium to narrow after the two-way conversion channel opens at the end of July following the release of SEC F-6 and other regulatory documents, the significant price difference still creates excellent opportunities for arbitrageurs until then. The root cause of the premium in the crypto market is the overflow of "bottom-fishing" sentiment. Since many players cannot directly open accounts with Korean brokers to trade Korean stocks, they can only turn their attention to Hynix's perpetual contracts listed on crypto exchanges. During last week's sharp correction, a large number of retail investors flocked to Binance and Hyperliquid to "bottom-fish" with perpetual contracts, causing the platforms' funding rates to soar. Over the past 30 days, the annualized funding rate (APR) of Hynix perpetual contracts on cryptocurrency trading platforms has mostly remained above 30%. Furthermore, time zone discrepancies have led to temporary pricing misalignments. During the closure of the Korean and US stock markets, perpetual contracts in the cryptocurrency market lack external price anchors and rely primarily on index-weighted moving averages (EWMA) for pricing. This can cause price "front-running" or lag, creating arbitrage opportunities. Arbitrage Strategy Diversification: From "Arbitrage" to "Fixed Income" Significant pricing discrepancies in the same underlying asset across different markets have created abundant arbitrage opportunities. As participants continue to enter the market, arbitrage strategies have rapidly evolved from the most basic "cross-market arbitrage" to complex combinations incorporating cryptocurrency tools. Strategy 1: Cross-Market Premium Convergence Game The most classic strategy is to "buy Korean spot stocks + short US ADRs," betting on future price convergence. Assuming a premium of over 35% between US ADRs and Korean spot stocks, users can buy Korean spot stocks through Interactive Brokers (IBKR) while simultaneously shorting SKHY. Arbitrage trader yourQuantGuy points out that the main cost of this strategy is short selling interest: SKHY's annualized short selling interest initially reached as high as 50%, but quickly declined to the 2%-5% range as supply increased. Combined with the fact that the combined margin account's long and short positions barely tie up capital, the holding cost will be significantly reduced. Closing the position in batches when the premium falls below 30% can achieve a return of over 4%. It's important to be wary that this isn't risk-free arbitrage, but rather a risk-reward trade based on "betting on premium convergence." Arbitrageurs generally assume the premium between Korean spot stocks and US ADRs will revert to its previous level, but referring to TSMC's precedent: even with the opening of swap channels, ADR premiums may become the norm. Hynix ADRs also have an issuance cap, and retail conversion has procedural hurdles. If the supply release after the two-way conversion begins falls short of expectations, the premium may become a long-term phenomenon, and positions betting on convergence will face continuous floating losses. Strategy Two: Cash & Futures Arbitrage + Fee Harvesting With major crypto trading platforms successively listing Hynix perpetual contracts, on-chain trading has become one of the most active battlegrounds for arbitrage. Buy Korean stock spot through a brokerage firm, and simultaneously short a perpetual contract of equal value on Binance or Hyperliquid. The spot long position and the contract short position hedge against stock price volatility risk, but the short position will receive high funding fees paid by the long position. Strategy Three: Arbitrage through Differences in Trading Platform Rules + "EWMA Mechanism" Due to differences in index compilation rules and funding fee settlement mechanisms among major cryptocurrency trading platforms, price and fee differences generally exist between platforms. Index calculation rules during non-trading hours: Binance uses the EWMA index to calculate the mark price, making it easy for contracts to "rush" to profit from price differences before the market opens; Hyperliquid references pre-market and post-market auction prices; OKX uses a proportional combination of the indices of the former two as its pricing benchmark. Arbitrage trader Sanfen stated that rule differences have led to the norm of "Binance perpetual contract price > OKX > Hyperliquid". A loophole in the Cap mechanism exists: Binance's single-transaction funding rate cap (Cap) was once locked at 0.5% (8 hours), while Hyperliquid settles hourly with no cap, causing the Hynix contract price spread to widen to $30. "Smart money" traders made hundreds of thousands of dollars in a short period by "shorting Binance + going long Hyperliquid". However, the funding rules, index components, and settlement frequencies of crypto trading platforms are not fixed. In mid-July, Binance changed the Hynix perpetual contract funding rate from 8-hour settlement to 4-hour settlement, causing the price spread to narrow by nearly half within half a day. Arbitrage strategies relying on stable mechanisms need to constantly pay attention to rule changes. Strategy 4: Cross-Market Derivatives + ETF Arbitrage Last Friday (July 17th), during the period when the Korean stock market was closed and the Hong Kong stock market was trading normally, the Hong Kong stock 2x long Hynix ETF experienced a discount of over 20% due to panic selling. Traders could hedge their exposure by buying the discounted ETF and shorting the on-chain perpetual contract, and then close their positions after the discount narrowed when the Korean stock market opened. Strategy 5: Interest Rate Derivatives to Lock in "Delta-Neutral Fixed Income" On July 20th, Pendle's structured interest rate platform Boros launched the funding rate market for Hyperliquid Hynix perpetual contracts, allowing users to convert their previously floating funding rates into fixed rates, completing a key link in the arbitrage ecosystem. The market has quickly developed Delta-neutral fixed-income strategies: 1. Spot Leg: Buy Hynix Korean stock on traditional brokerages like IBKR; 2. Contract Leg: Open an equivalent value of short Hynix perpetual contracts on Hyperliquid, collecting floating funding rates; 3. Fixed Leg: Short YU on Boros (i.e., sell floating rates), locking in approximately 30% fixed income. The spot and perpetual contracts achieve Delta neutrality, with the two floating rate legs offsetting each other, resulting in a net profit from the fixed rates on the Boros side. However, the liquidity of SK Hynix-related assets, whether in Boros' interest rate market or Hyperliquid's order book, remains somewhat weaker compared to traditional stock markets. Large positions can easily lead to significant price differences and slippage losses when entering and exiting trades, lowering the overall net return of the strategy. Additionally, cross-border transaction costs are worth noting. Hidden costs such as fluctuations in the Korean won exchange rate, changes in securities lending interest rates, and the efficiency of cross-border settlement can continuously erode profits, making it difficult for ordinary investors to cover all risks. Even so, as long as fees and price differences exist, arbitrageurs will not leave the market. The cross-market arbitrage frenzy surrounding SK Hynix is ŌĆŗŌĆŗalso a microcosm of the further intertwining of traditional financial assets and DeFi infrastructure. Institutional barriers in traditional markets are rapidly broken down on the blockchain into multiple layers of trading tools, such as spot price spreads, cross-platform fee spreads, and interest rate derivatives, creating a richer trading dimension and higher capital efficiency than traditional markets. At the end of July, with the approach of Hynix's ADR swap window and earnings report date, the convergence of these two events may become a key turning point in the premium trend. This arbitrage game spanning multiple markets may present another fascinating picture.

data.title: When ETH is deeply embedded in the balance sheet: Where is staking headed next?
data.description:
Author: imToken

Currently, if you want to become an Ethereum validator directly, you have to wait in line for over a month.

As of July 22, there were still approximately 2.5 million ETH in the Ethereum staking queue, with an estimated waiting time of over 43 days. In contrast, the waiting time to exit the queue is only a few minutes, almost negligible.

From the numbers alone, there is no doubt that the increasing amount of ETH staking is siphoning off the market's circulating supply.

But what's more noteworthy than the growth in staking volume is that the queuing process is beginning to become a matter of capital efficiency. After all, for ETH treasury companies and institutions that choose the native staking path, a queuing period of over 40 days means that a considerable amount of assets cannot generate staking rewards temporarily, and asset allocation, liquidity arrangements, and opportunity costs all need to be recalculated.

Ultimately, as ETH is more deeply integrated into the balance sheet, the problem facing staking is no longer just "how to get more people to participate," but is beginning to become a more traditional and complex asset management issue.

I. Record High Staking Ratio: How to Understand the Queuing?

Ethereum's current high staking ratio did not suddenly appear at a certain point in time.

In 2023, Shapela (Shanghai + Capella) upgraded and opened the staking withdrawal function, allowing validators to retrieve their staked principal and rewards at the protocol level. This created a relatively complete entry, operation, and exit loop for ETH staking. Subsequently, the derivatives market based on LSD expanded rapidly, driving the ETH staking ratio higher and higher.

As of the time of writing, the amount of ETH staked has exceeded 40 million, worth approximately $140 billion at the current price, accounting for more than 33% of the total supply. This is a significant increase compared to the staking ratio of about 10% a few years ago, setting a new historical high.

In other words, currently, more than 1 out of every 3 ETH is being staked. With the staking rate reaching new highs, the persistently high entry queue has also exposed a new problem. As is well known, Ethereum's entry and exit queues are essentially a rate-limiting mechanism to protect consensus stability. New ETH cannot enter the validator set indefinitely, nor can exits occur in a concentrated manner within a short period. The protocol sets the number of ETH that can be processed per Epoch based on the current validator size. When the funds applying to enter or exit exceed the processing capacity, a queue will form. From this perspective, the 2.5 million ETH queuing to enter firstly indicates that the market demand for staking capacity far exceeds the protocol's current release rateŌĆöthis could include newly entering long-term funds, treasury companies deploying existing holdings, staking service providers adjusting their validator structure, and institutions transferring ETH from their escrow accounts to the staking system. Therefore, this actually sends a clear signal: at least at the current stage, the funds willing to let ETH enter the staking system far exceed the funds actively withdrawing from the validator set. This is significantly different from the staking logic at the initial launch of Beacon Chain. Early ETH staking was more like a network participation mechanism for technical users, independent validators, and long-term Ethereum supporters. Participants ran nodes, maintained the network, and assumed technical risks in exchange for protocol rewards. With the rise of liquidity staking, it gradually became a product for ordinary token holders to obtain on-chain rewards. For example, exchange staking, staking as a service, and staking pools lowered the technical barrier. Liquidity staking protocols such as Lido and Rocket Pool further increased the availability of staking funds, allowing users to obtain liquidity staking tokens such as stETH and rETH after staking ETH. These tokens can be transferred and traded, and can also be used in lending, liquidity pools, and other DeFi protocols. Today, with a large amount of ETH entering corporate treasuries, fund products, and professional custody systems, staking is undoubtedly moving towards its third stage, shifting from "who can participate in staking" to "how large-scale ETH should be managed." Of course, the institutionalization mentioned here does not mean that early staking was entirely dominated by retail investors, nor does it mean that institutions will replace ordinary users. More accurately, the focus of market discussion is changing: In the past, the focus was on how ordinary users could obtain staking rewards. Now, the focus is on how staking can become a standardized financial management capability once hundreds of thousands or even millions of ETH enter a company's balance sheet.

II. Structural Changes Behind Institutions Like BitMine

The emergence of ETH Treasury companies is making this change even more apparent.

Because the core logic of Bitcoin Treasury companies is to continuously accumulate BTC through financing and capital market operations, increasing the number of Bitcoins corresponding to each share; but for ETH Treasury companies, holding assets is not the end of the strategy.

This is because the core logic of Bitcoin Treasury companies is to continuously accumulate BTC through financing and capital market operations, increasing the number of Bitcoins corresponding to each share; but for ETH Treasury companies, holding assets is not the end of the strategy.

After all, BTC itself doesn't offer native staking rewards. Holders typically need to introduce lending, custody, derivatives, or other counterparty risks to obtain additional returns. ETH, on the other hand, can directly participate in Ethereum consensus, earning protocol rewards without selling assets. This naturally adds an extra layer of operational space to the ETH treasury; besides deciding how much ETH to buy, it also needs to decide how that ETH will be deployed. BitMine's actions are a prime example of this institutional approach. According to its latest disclosed data, as of July 19, BitMine held a total of 5,777,468 ETH, accounting for approximately 4.8% of the total ETH supply. Of this, 4.917 million ETH were staked, representing 85% of its total ETH holdings, valued at approximately $9.2 billion. Based on the ETH price at the time and BitMine's own 2.67% seven-day annualized staking yield, the company expected to earn approximately $247 million in staking income annually; if all of its ETH were eventually staked, the annualized reward could reach approximately $290 million. More noteworthy is the speed at which this figure is changing. In early February of this year, BitMine had approximately 2.8975 million ETH staked, representing about 67% of its holdings at the time. By mid-July, this had increased to approximately 4.9172 million ETH. In other words, in less than six months, BitMine deployed over 2 million new ETH, raising its staking coverage ratio from approximately two-thirds to 85%. This demonstrates that Tom Lee and BitMine are visibly staking their ETH holdings, transforming it from a mere crypto asset waiting for price appreciation into a fundamental on-chain asset with inherent yield potential. For ordinary investors, the staking ratio might simply be a yield option, but for BitMine, it is becoming a key treasury performance indicator alongside ETH holdings, net asset value per share, and financing costs. Meanwhile, BitMine also launched its own institutional-grade staking platform, MAVAN, to serve its own ETH treasury. It also plans to provide staking infrastructure to institutional investors, custodians, and ecosystem partners in the future. This means that staking serves at least three roles for BitMine: First, it adds an ETH-based return layer to long-term holdings; second, staking rewards can be reinvested, increasing the amount of ETH held in the treasury; and finally, when its self-built validator capabilities are opened to the outside world, the staking infrastructure itself may become a service business. SharpLink further extends this logic from native staking to active yield management. For it, basic staking returns are just the starting point; some of the staked ETH can continue to enter the on-chain yield fund and be allocated to DeFi strategies such as liquidity and lending. The changes in Lido V3 occur at the infrastructure layer. Previously, users and institutions primarily entered unified liquidity staking pools. Now, institutions can use more independent staking vaults, choosing their own node operators, fee structures, and risk parameters, while retaining the option to obtain stETH liquidity. This means that liquidity staking is moving from a standardized product towards an isolated, customizable institutional-grade infrastructure. Therefore, the competition among ETH treasury companies may not only be about who holds more, but also about who can manage these ETH at lower costs, with higher uptime, and more robust risk control. From this perspective, ETH is also transforming from a crypto asset waiting for price increases into an asset requiring continuous operation. III. Why is Staking More Important When the Yield Isn't High? As of the time of writing, the Ethereum network's staking APR is approximately 2.64%. Realistically speaking, compared to some DeFi products, this level is not particularly outstanding, and as the amount of ETH staked continues to increase, this basic yield may be further diluted. However, institutional demand for staking cannot be understood solely in terms of yieldŌĆöstaking reduces their opportunity cost of holding ETH long-term. For short-term investors, an annualized return of 2% to 3% is unlikely to offset the price volatility of ETH itself. But for treasury companies, funds, or large addresses that have decided to hold ETH long-term, the issue is that since ETH is already on their balance sheets, they must continuously acquire more ETH through network security participation without giving up their ETH price exposure. This is easy to understand. For an ordinary user holding 100 ETH, a 2.6% return might not be significant; however, for a treasury firm holding millions of ETH, the same return would generate substantial absolute income, gradually influencing the number of ETH per share through long-term reinvestment. This is also one of the key differences between ETH and BTC in their treasury narratives. Therefore, when ETH enters an institution's balance sheet, the treasury department is not dealing with a static position, but rather an on-chain asset that can be continuously deployed, accounted for, and adjusted. Furthermore, as institutional participation increases, the native staking yield may also assume another function: becoming the yield benchmark for the entire ETH asset system. This is easy to understand. For example, in the future, when a DeFi strategy promises returns of 5%, 8%, or even higher, institutions won't be comparing "having a return" or "not having a return," but rather how much more it earns relative to the native staking return of approximately 2.6%, and what additional risks it takes on. Lending, liquidity making, structured products, and restaking strategies all need to demonstrate the reasonableness of their risk-reward ratio based on this fundamental return. From this perspective, the importance of the next stage of staking lies not only in how much ETH it brings to token holders, but also in its becoming the underlying benchmark for measuring other on-chain strategies. However, it cannot be simply regarded as Ethereum's "risk-free rate." Stakers need to bear risks such as ETH price fluctuations, validator offline events, node failures, and potential penalties. Participation through service providers will also increase the risks for operators and custodians. If it continues to enter DeFi, the risks will be compounded with the increase in the number of protocol and strategy layers. In addition, higher staking ratios do not only have positive effects. If new funds are mainly concentrated in the hands of a few treasury companies, custodians, liquidity staking protocols, and node operators, it is likely to exacerbate the centralization of validators, cloud service providers, and jurisdictions. Therefore, as staking evolves from a network participation mechanism into an institutional asset allocation tool, Ethereum needs to address not only how to absorb more funds, but also how to maintain a balance between capital efficiency, institutional demand, and decentralization. In conclusion, from the initial 32 ETH requirement for validator nodes at the launch of Beacon Chain, to the liquidity staking protocol lowering the participation threshold, and now to treasury companies, self-built validator networks, and institutional on-chain yield funds, the changes in staking essentially reflect the market's changing understanding of ETH. It initially served as a mechanism for participating in network consensus, then became a tool for ordinary users to obtain on-chain rewards, and is now entering company balance sheets, custody systems, and professional yield management frameworks. For these long-term holders, a 2% to 3% return may not be impressive. However, as long as ETH is no longer confined to addresses or custodian accounts waiting for its price to rise, but can participate in cybersecurity, earn protocol rewards, and continuously reinvest, while maintaining a certain level of liquidity, it will help accelerate its transformation into an underlying asset for other financial strategies. This is also the new era's challenge for ETH.

data.title: A massive $13.58 million long position in SPCX and the SEC is propelling US stocks toward all-day trading.
data.description:
Headline

Ō¢īA whale heavily invested $13.58 million in SPCX, with an average entry price of $126.04

On July 23, according to Onchain Lens monitoring, Hyperliquid whale 0x3527ŌĆ”1e16 held a long position of 122,000 SPCX, worth approximately $13.58 million. The current average entry price was $126.04, while the market price has fallen to $111.29, resulting in a current unrealized loss of approximately $1.8 million. The liquidation price is $22.27.

Ō¢īUS SEC Pushes for 24-Hour Trading Roundtable to Hold 24-Hour Trading

On July 23, the US Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) announced that it will hold a roundtable meeting at its Washington headquarters on September 17, 2026, to discuss issues related to the transition of the US stock market to 24-hour trading, including preparations to support overnight trading, operations and resilience in a 24-hour market, and the opportunities and challenges of expanding trading hours. SEC Chairman Paul Atkins stated in a press release, "We are moving towards a new dayŌĆöand a new nightŌĆöfor the US stock market. With the expansion to overnight trading, I am excited to see the US stock market align with those markets that have already achieved continuous trading, and I look forward to achieving a balance between 24-hour trading and the crucial protection of investors and clients."

... (Golden Encyclopedia) Can Bitcoin Still Be Called "Digital Gold" During Geopolitical Crises? Every geopolitical crisis causes gold prices to rise while Bitcoin prices plummet. After six tests, the claim of "digital gold" has never been substantiated by data. Countries hoard gold but exclude Bitcoin from their reserves. For investors, Bitcoin exhibits asymmetry: it falls with stocks but doesn't rise with them. Three structural asymmetries prevent Bitcoin from achieving safe-haven status: an overabundance of derivatives (market structure), the dominance of leveraged traders (participant composition), and a lack of repeatable behavioral records (behavioral accumulation). Bitcoin is not a safe-haven asset, but it is a "useful asset in times of crisis," and it can indeed function when borders are closed and banks fail. If these three asymmetries narrow, Bitcoin may no longer be a replica of gold, but rather a completely new "next-generation gold." Generational shifts and the widespread application of algorithms are key factors that could accelerate this process.
